skill.name: multi-agent-patterns
skill.description: Design multi-agent systems that split complex work across coordinated agents, with guidance for orchestration, handoffs, consensus, and failure handling.
skill.summary: Plan reliable multi-agent architectures with practical coordination patterns.
skill.author: Asmayaseen

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many high and critical signals, but most are false positives from markdown examples, ordinary words such as description, and dictionary key access. The confirmed concern is that reference examples show filesystem coordination with unsanitized path construction, which could become path traversal or unintended file overwrite if copied into production code without validation.

medium_findings.title: Unsafe Filesystem Path Construction in Reference Examples
medium_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE. The filesystem coordination examples build file paths from workspace, key, resource, and workflow identifiers before reading or writing files. This is documentation code rather than active malicious behavior, but copied implementations could allow path traversal or unintended overwrite without path normalization and allowlists.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file write and directory creation patterns are directly present in reference examples. Confidence is moderated because they are illustrative snippets, not executed by the skill itself.

medium_findings.title: Full Context Delegation and Direct Response Patterns Need Redaction Controls
medium_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE. The skill recommends direct sub-agent message forwarding and full context delegation patterns. These patterns can be legitimate, but production systems need redaction, authorization checks, and output validation to avoid leaking private context across agents or directly to users.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The semantic risk is clear from the described architecture patterns, but the skill also includes general validation guidance elsewhere. This makes the issue a design warning rather than evidence of malicious intent.

low_findings.title: External Command Static Findings Are Markdown and Example False Positives
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE. The reported Ruby or shell backtick execution hits are markdown fences, text diagrams, and Python framework examples. I did not find evidence of shell execution, subprocess use, os.system, or command strings in the reviewed files.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged locations are inside markdown code fences or diagrams, and targeted search found no executable shell invocation APIs. This strongly supports dismissal of the external command category.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography and Sensitive File Static Findings Are Lexical False Positives
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE. The weak cryptography findings align with ordinary words such as design and description, and the sensitive file finding maps to dictionary key access. I did not find evidence of cryptographic algorithms, certificate files, private keys, or credential handling.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines contain prose, task descriptions, and results.keys() usage. No imports or operations for cryptography, certificates, private keys, or credential storage were present.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded Network URLs Are Documentation Links
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE. The network findings are external resource links in the References section. The skill does not include code that performs HTTP requests or transmits data to those URLs.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs appear only in markdown reference links. Search did not find HTTP client calls or network execution code in the executable scripts.

dangerous_patterns.title: Path Construction Followed by File Write
dangerous_patterns.description: Reference examples concatenate caller-provided identifiers into paths and then write files. Production versions should resolve paths under an approved workspace and reject traversal characters.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The pattern is explicit in the documentation examples, including path construction and write operations. It is less severe because no active skill entry point executes it automatically.

dangerous_patterns.title: Direct Agent Output Forwarding
dangerous_patterns.description: The direct forwarding pattern can bypass supervisor review. It should be paired with output validation and privacy filtering before user delivery.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The risk follows from the described pass-through design. Confidence is moderate because the sample is architectural guidance, not a complete implementation.

content.value_statement: Complex AI tasks often exceed one agent context or need specialized workflows. This skill helps Claude, Codex, and Claude Code plan coordinated multi-agent systems with clearer handoffs and failure controls.
actual_capabilities: Explains supervisor, swarm, and hierarchical multi-agent architecture patterns.
Guides task decomposition for agents with separate context windows.
Describes handoff protocols, direct response forwarding, and state passing.
Outlines weighted voting, debate, and convergence checks for consensus.
Provides failure handling concepts such as circuit breakers, retries, and checkpointing.
Compares implementation approaches for LangGraph, AutoGen, and CrewAI-style systems.
limitations: It provides architecture guidance, not a ready production agent platform.
Example code needs security hardening before use with untrusted input.
It does not benchmark your specific models, tools, or latency costs.
It does not replace privacy review for workflows that share user data between agents.

use_cases.title: Plan a Research Agent Team
use_cases.description: Design a supervisor-led workflow that separates search, analysis, verification, and writing across specialized agents.

use_cases.title: Choose an Agent Topology
use_cases.description: Compare supervisor, swarm, and hierarchy patterns for a product workflow with clear coordination and failure boundaries.

use_cases.title: Harden Agent Collaboration
use_cases.description: Identify risks from handoffs, shared state, context leakage, and weak consensus before implementation starts.

prompt_templates.title: Select a Basic Pattern
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the multi-agent-patterns skill to recommend one architecture for this task: [describe task]. Explain why it fits and when a single agent would be better.

prompt_templates.title: Decompose a Workflow
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the multi-agent-patterns skill to split this workflow into agents, responsibilities, inputs, outputs, and handoff rules: [workflow details].

prompt_templates.title: Design Coordination Controls
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the multi-agent-patterns skill to design consensus, retry, timeout, and circuit breaker controls for this multi-agent system: [system description].

prompt_templates.title: Review a Production Design
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the multi-agent-patterns skill to review this multi-agent design for context isolation, privacy leaks, supervisor bottlenecks, divergence, and unsafe shared state: [design].

output_examples.input: A product team needs agents for market research, competitive analysis, and final report writing.
output_examples.output: A supervisor pattern with researcher, analyst, fact-checker, and writer agents, plus explicit handoff schemas and final validation gates.

output_examples.input: A support workflow needs flexible routing between billing, technical, and account teams.
output_examples.output: A swarm-style handoff design with a triage agent, bounded transfer rules, shared state limits, and escalation checks.

output_examples.input: A company wants to prevent agents from agreeing on weak answers during planning.
output_examples.output: A debate protocol with weighted confidence, independent critiques, convergence checks, and a supervisor review step before final decisions.

best_practices: Start with one agent, then add agents only when context isolation or parallel work is clearly useful.
Define each handoff with required inputs, expected outputs, privacy limits, and failure behavior.
Validate agent outputs before downstream agents or users rely on them.
anti_patterns: Creating many role-based agents without a clear context or workflow boundary.
Using majority voting when agents share the same weak evidence or prompts.
Passing full context between agents when a smaller task-specific summary is enough.

faq.question: When should I use this skill?
faq.answer: Use it when a task exceeds one agent context, needs parallel subtasks, or requires specialized agent roles.

faq.question: Does this skill run a multi-agent system?
faq.answer: No. It provides architecture guidance, reference patterns, and example coordination code.

faq.question: Which frameworks does it discuss?
faq.answer: It discusses LangGraph, AutoGen, and CrewAI-style patterns at a practical architecture level.

faq.question: Can I use the examples in production?
faq.answer: Use them as starting points only. Add input validation, path safety, authorization, monitoring, and tests first.

faq.question: How does it help with context limits?
faq.answer: It shows how to split work across agents so each agent carries only the context needed for its task.

faq.question: What is the main security concern?
faq.answer: Shared state, full context delegation, and direct forwarding can expose private data if safeguards are missing.
